items.question_text: In the clearance-item order listed in 4-2-1, what appears between altitude data and holding instructions?
items.explanation: The order is: (e) Altitude data in the order flown, then (f) Mach number if applicable, then (g) USAF transmission limitations, then (h) Holding instructions. Between altitude data and holding instructions, Mach number and the USAF limitation note appear.

choices.text: Holding instructions come directly after altitude data.
Frequency and beacon code information.
Mach number, if applicable.
Any special information.

items.question_text: When issuing a clearance to an airborne USAF aircraft with an altitude assignment, you may include the altitude plus no more than one of which five items in the same transmission?
items.explanation: The five listed items are: frequency change, transponder change, heading, altimeter setting, and traffic information containing an altitude. No more than one of these may accompany the altitude assignment in the same transmission.

choices.text: frequency change, transponder change, heading, altimeter setting, traffic information containing an altitude

items.question_text: When a clearance limit is an intersection or waypoint and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.explanation: Per 4-2-1b3, when the clearance limit is an intersection or waypoint and the type is known, the type must follow the intersection or waypoint name, e.g., 'Cleared to HANEY intersection.'

choices.text: The word 'airport' after the name.
The frequency of the defining NAVAID.
The type of intersection or waypoint.
The latitude/longitude coordinates.

items.question_text: When an aircraft is cleared to climb via a SID with published altitude restrictions, and the top altitude is changed to FL230 while compliance with the altitude restrictions is still required, what phraseology is used?
items.explanation: The example in 4-2-5b2 shows: 'Climb via SID except maintain Flight Level Two Three Zero.' This preserves the SID altitude restrictions while changing only the top altitude.

choices.text: Amend altitude, climb and maintain Flight Level Two Three Zero.
Climb via SID except maintain Flight Level Two Three Zero.
Cancel SID restrictions, climb and maintain Flight Level Two Three Zero.
Climb via SID to Flight Level Two Three Zero.

items.question_text: According to Note 2 of 4-2-5, can ATC cancel or amend crossing altitudes and speed restrictions on Obstacle Departure Procedures?
items.explanation: Note 2 states crossing altitudes and speed restrictions on Obstacle Departure Procedures (ODPs) cannot be canceled or amended by ATC. This is because ODPs provide essential terrain and obstacle protection that ATC lacks the data to independently verify.

choices.text: Yes, if the aircraft is radar identified.
No, they cannot be canceled or amended by ATC.
Yes, but only with pilot concurrence.
Yes, only after the aircraft reaches the MEA.

items.question_text: What effort should ATC specialists make regarding NRP aircraft after tactically resolving a conflict with route or altitude changes?
items.explanation: The note to 4-2-5d states controllers should make every effort to ensure the aircraft is returned to the original filed flight plan/altitude as soon as conditions warrant, after tactically resolving a conflict.

choices.text: Return the aircraft to the original filed flight plan and altitude as soon as conditions warrant.
Keep the aircraft on the tactical routing for the remainder of the flight.
Ask the pilot to file a new flight plan reflecting the tactical change.
Transfer the aircraft to the next sector without restoring the original route.

items.question_text: When an aircraft changes from VFR to IFR and is Mode-C equipped, the controller must assign a beacon code that will allow what function?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-8c requires assigning a beacon code to Mode-C equipped aircraft that will allow MSAW (Minimum Safe Altitude Warning) alarms when changing from VFR to IFR.

choices.text: Conflict alert functions.
MSAW alarms.
Mode S interrogation.
ADS-B tracking only.

items.instruction: Match the clearance item to its position in the standard order.
items.question_text: What appears as item (d) in the standard clearance-item order, between SID/vectors and altitude data?

choices.text: Mach number, if applicable.
Route of flight including ADR/ADAR/AAR when applied.
Holding instructions.
Frequency and beacon code information.

items.explanation: The order in 4-2-1 is: (a) Aircraft ID, (b) Clearance limit, (c) SID or vectors, (d) Route of flight including ADR/ADAR/AAR, (e) Altitude data, (f) Mach number, (g) USAF limit, (h) Holding, (i) Special info.

items.instruction: Select the correctly formatted clearance limit for each type.
items.question_text: Which clearance-limit phrase is correct for a clearance limit of the RENO intersection when the type is known?

choices.text: Cleared to RENO.
Cleared intersection RENO.
Cleared to RENO intersection.
Cleared to RENO waypoint airport.

items.explanation: When the clearance limit is an intersection and the type is known, the type must follow the name: 'Cleared to RENO intersection.'

items.instruction: Select the statement that correctly reflects the amendment rules.
items.question_text: When a controller restates 'maintain' altitude in an amended clearance and omits previously issued altitude restrictions, what is the effect?

choices.text: Those previously issued altitude restrictions are canceled, including SID/STAR altitude restrictions if any.
The restrictions remain in effect because the pilot has them on the chart.
Only speed restrictions are canceled, but altitude crossing restrictions remain.
Only SID restrictions are canceled, but STAR restrictions remain.

items.explanation: Note 1 of 4-2-5b: restating maintain altitude and omitting previously issued altitude restrictions cancels them, including any SID/STAR altitude restrictions. This is why controllers must restate all applicable restrictions when amending.

items.situation: An NRP aircraft displaying 'NRP' in its flight plan remarks has an amended clearance with a different route and altitude to tactically resolve a potential conflict. The conflict has been resolved and the original flight plan route is now available.
items.para_context: ATC specialists should avoid route and/or altitude changes for NRP aircraft. Every effort should be made to ensure the aircraft is returned to the original filed flight plan/altitude as soon as conditions warrant.
items.question_text: After the conflict is resolved, what is the appropriate action?

choices.text: Keep the aircraft on the amended clearance for the rest of the flight to avoid confusion.
Return the aircraft to its original filed flight plan route and altitude as soon as conditions warrant.
Ask the pilot whether they prefer the original or amended route.
Transfer NRP handling to the next sector without restoring the original clearance.

items.explanation: The note to 4-2-5d directs controllers to make every effort to return the NRP aircraft to the original filed flight plan and altitude as soon as conditions warrant after conflict resolution.
